description: Versioned registry of Google Ads policy and behavior assumptions used across toprank ads skills. Each entry tracks a specific assumption, its volatility, verification source, and when it should be re-verified.

entries.rule: Ad Rank = f(Bid, Quality Score, Expected Impact of Extensions, Ad Rank Thresholds, Auction Context). This is the core auction equation governing ad placement.
entries.notes: The components feeding into Ad Rank evolve (e.g., context signals expand), but the fundamental equation structure has been stable for years.

entries.rule: Google Ads object hierarchy: Account > Campaign > Ad Group > Ad/Keyword. Budget and bid strategy are set at campaign level; match types, bids, and ads at ad group level.
entries.notes: PMax campaigns flatten this hierarchy (no ad groups in the traditional sense), but the core hierarchy remains for Search campaigns.

entries.rule: CTR = Clicks / Impressions. CPC = Cost / Clicks. Conversion Rate = Conversions / Clicks. CPA = Cost / Conversions. ROAS = Conversion Value / Cost.
entries.notes: Definitions are stable. What counts as a 'conversion' depends on the account's conversion action setup and attribution model.

entries.rule: GAQL (Google Ads Query Language) uses SQL-like SELECT/FROM/WHERE/ORDER BY/LIMIT syntax against resource types (campaign, ad_group, ad_group_criterion, etc.).
entries.notes: New fields and resources are added with API versions, but the query syntax itself is stable. Field names may change across API versions.

entries.rule: RSA character limits: Headlines max 30 chars (up to 15 per RSA), Descriptions max 90 chars (up to 4 per RSA). Display paths max 15 chars each. Minimum: 3 headlines, 2 descriptions.
entries.notes: Character limits have not changed since RSA introduction. Google may expand max headlines/descriptions in the future.

entries.rule: Quality Score (1-10) is composed of three sub-components: Expected CTR, Ad Relevance, and Landing Page Experience. Each rated Below Average / Average / Above Average.
entries.notes: The three-component structure has been consistent. QS is a keyword-level metric. The internal weighting of components is not published by Google.

entries.rule: Conversion tracking is a prerequisite for all Smart Bidding strategies (tCPA, tROAS, Maximize Conversions, Maximize Conversion Value). Without it, only Manual CPC, Maximize Clicks, and Target Impression Share are available.
entries.notes: This fundamental requirement is unlikely to change. Enhanced conversions and server-side tagging are expanding the ways to track, but the requirement itself is stable.

entries.rule: Daily budget is set at the campaign level. Google may spend up to 2x the daily budget on any given day but will not exceed 30.4x the daily budget in a calendar month (monthly spending limit).
entries.notes: The 2x daily / 30.4x monthly overdelivery policy has been stable. Shared budgets across campaigns are also available.

entries.rule: Search IS, top IS, abs top IS, budget-lost IS, and rank-lost IS are standard metrics available via GAQL and the Google Ads UI.
entries.notes: These metrics have been stable across API versions. Field names may change between API versions.

entries.rule: Approximate QS component weights: Expected CTR ~35-40%, Ad Relevance ~20-25%, Landing Page Experience ~35-40%. Landing Page + Expected CTR together account for ~70-80% of QS.
entries.notes: Google has never published official weights. These are estimates from third-party regression studies (WordStream, Optmyzr). Use directionally, not as exact figures.

entries.rule: QS CPC impact relative to QS 5 baseline: QS 10 = ~50% discount, QS 7 = ~28% discount, QS 3 = ~67% premium, QS 1 = ~400% premium. Rule of thumb: +1 QS above 5 saves ~16% CPC; -1 below 5 costs ~50% more.
entries.notes: Approximate estimates from WordStream and Optmyzr studies. Actual CPC modifiers vary by auction, vertical, and account history. Not Google-published data.

entries.rule: Pinning RSA headlines/descriptions to specific positions reduces the number of combinations Google can test, which may lower ad strength and reduce serving. Pin only when regulatory or brand requirements demand it.
entries.notes: Google's RSA rotation algorithm evolves. The trade-off between pinning control and combination reduction remains, but the severity of the penalty may shift.

entries.rule: A/B testing minimum: at least 30 clicks/day per variant for meaningful data. Below 30 clicks/day total across all campaigns, avoid A/B tests — statistical significance takes weeks. Allow 2-4 weeks minimum for any test.
entries.notes: The 30 clicks/day threshold is a practical guideline, not a Google-enforced rule. Lower volume accounts need longer test windows.

entries.rule: Current best practice: themed ad groups with 10-20 closely themed keywords per ad group. SKAGs (single keyword ad groups) are detrimental — LLM-based bidding needs signal density from keyword clusters.
entries.notes: This shifted from SKAG (2012-2017) to STAG (2017-2020) to themed (2020+). Google's recommendation engine and smart bidding perform better with fewer, larger ad groups.

entries.rule: Theme definition: keywords should share the same intent AND could be answered by the same landing page. 2-3 RSAs per ad group. 1 primary landing page per ad group. Exact + broad match is the standard pairing.
entries.notes: The exact+broad pairing reflects phrase and broad match now overlapping ~100% in many verticals, making phrase match redundant alongside broad.

entries.rule: For brand campaigns, use Target Impression Share bidding targeting 95%+ top of page. Set max CPC cap to prevent runaway costs. Brand campaigns should get 5-10% of total budget.
entries.notes: The strategy itself is stable, but optimal impression share targets may shift with competitive dynamics and SERP layout changes.

entries.rule: Negative keywords do NOT expand to close variants, synonyms, or paraphrases like positive keywords do. Negative exact blocks only that exact term. Negative phrase blocks queries containing the exact phrase. Must add negatives for each variant explicitly.
entries.notes: This asymmetry between positive and negative match types is a frequent source of wasted spend. Google could change this behavior, which would significantly alter negative keyword strategy.

entries.rule: Google Consent Mode v2 is required for EEA/UK traffic to maintain conversion measurement and remarketing. Must implement consent_mode with ad_storage and analytics_storage parameters. Without it, conversion data gaps in EU markets.
entries.notes: Privacy regulations evolve. Consent Mode implementation requirements may expand to other regions. Google may add new consent signal types.

entries.rule: Google applies close variants (misspellings, plurals, stemming, synonyms, paraphrases, implied words) to ALL match types including exact match. Exact match keywords can match to synonyms and paraphrases Google considers same-intent.
entries.notes: Close variant expansion has been one-directional (always expanding). The scope of what constitutes a 'close variant' grows over time. Exact match is no longer truly exact.

entries.rule: Standard migration path: New campaign -> Maximize Conversions (2-4 weeks) -> Target CPA (once learning exits) -> Target ROAS (if value tracking active and values vary 3x+). Allow direct tCPA if account has cross-campaign conversion history.
entries.notes: Google increasingly encourages starting with automated bidding from day one. The rigid step-by-step ladder may become unnecessary as algorithms improve.

entries.rule: Display network baseline: CTR is typically 8-12x lower than Search CTR. CPC is typically 3-5x lower than Search CPC. Conversion rates are typically 3-4x lower than Search. Display is an awareness and remarketing channel, not direct response.
entries.notes: Display performance ratios relative to Search have been broadly consistent. Specific numbers shift with market conditions.

entries.rule: Impression share targets: Brand campaigns 90-95%+, Non-brand competitive 40-60%, Non-brand long-tail 60-80%, Competitor terms 20-40%. Brand IS < 80% is a red flag (competitors stealing brand traffic).
entries.notes: These are diagnostic benchmarks, not hard rules. Competitive dynamics and budget constraints affect achievable IS.

entries.rule: Splitting campaigns by match type (separate Exact, Phrase, Broad campaigns) is harmful. It fragments conversion data and starves Smart Bidding. Phrase and broad overlap ~100% in many verticals, making three-way splits pointless.
entries.notes: This was common practice pre-2020. The shift to consolidated structures reflects Smart Bidding's need for signal density.

entries.rule: Smart Bidding learning phase is now variable, not a fixed duration. Google states 'typically 1-2 weeks' but it depends on conversion volume, account history, and bid strategy. Do not judge results or make changes during the learning period.
entries.notes: Previously cited as a fixed 7-day learning phase. Google now uses variable duration based on multiple signals. The 2-week minimum wait advice is a practical guideline.

entries.rule: Broad match uses AI to determine intent and can match to themes, related concepts, and queries with no keyword words present (e.g., 'plumber services' matches 'fix leaky faucet'). Phrase and broad match overlap ~100% in many verticals (2025-2026).
entries.notes: Broad match reach continues to expand with Google's LLM improvements. The phrase-broad overlap may become complete, potentially making phrase match redundant.

entries.rule: Enhanced CPC (eCPC) was deprecated for new Search campaigns in 2024. Existing campaigns may still use it but Google no longer recommends it. Cannot be selected for new campaigns. Migrate to Maximize Conversions or Target CPA.
entries.notes: Google may fully sunset eCPC for existing campaigns at any time. Monitor for forced migration announcements.

entries.rule: Performance Max campaigns require asset groups with text assets (headlines, descriptions), images, videos, and logos. PMax serves across Search, Display, YouTube, Discover, Gmail, and Maps. PMax may serve text ads on Search inventory.
entries.notes: PMax capabilities and asset requirements change frequently. New asset types and placement options are added regularly. PMax search cannibalization behavior evolves.

entries.rule: Industry benchmarks (2024-2025 vintage): Legal CPC $6.75/CTR 4.4%/CPA $90, Home Services CPC $3.00/CTR 5.0%/CPA $37.50, E-commerce CPC $1.50/CTR 4.5%/CPA $37.50, SaaS CPC $4.20/CTR 3.8%/CPA $93. Well-managed accounts outperform averages by 30-50%.
entries.notes: Based on WordStream, Google, and LOCALiQ reports. CPCs rising 10-20% YoY due to increased competition and AI bidding adoption. Adjust 2024-2025 figures upward 10-20% for 2026.

entries.rule: Manual CPC forfeits access to Google's real-time LLM-based auction signals (device, location, time, audience, query intent, competitive density, browser, OS, and hundreds more). Not recommended for any new campaign.
entries.notes: The signal disadvantage grows as Google's bidding LLM improves. The gap between manual and automated bidding performance is widening over time.

entries.rule: PMax supports brand exclusions to prevent ads from showing on competitor brand terms or unwanted brand queries. Account-level brand suitability controls allow excluding specific brands from all campaign types.
entries.notes: Brand exclusion capabilities are expanding. Initially PMax-only, now spreading to other campaign types. Exact behavior and coverage evolving.

entries.rule: PMax Search Themes guide the algorithm toward specific search intents but reduce negative keyword precision — PMax negative keywords may not block as precisely as Search campaign negatives because the algorithm considers themes holistically.
entries.notes: Search Themes feature is relatively new. Its interaction with negative keywords and the degree of control it provides is still evolving.

entries.rule: Google Ads experiments use cookie-based traffic splitting (not search-based), meaning the same user consistently sees the same variant. This avoids cross-contamination but means split is user-based, not query-based.
entries.notes: With cookie deprecation discussions and privacy changes, the traffic splitting mechanism may change. Monitor for shifts to search-based or cohort-based splitting.

entries.rule: Campaign-level experiments allow A/B testing of bid strategies, ad copy, landing pages, and targeting. Campaign Mix Experiments for testing budget allocation across campaigns may have limited availability.
entries.notes: Google is expanding experiment capabilities. Multi-campaign experiments and portfolio-level testing features are in various stages of rollout.

entries.rule: Data-Driven Attribution (DDA) is the default and recommended attribution model. Google removed Last Click, First Click, Linear, Time Decay, and Position-Based models for new conversion actions (2023-2024). Existing actions may still use legacy models.
entries.notes: Google is moving toward DDA-only. Legacy models may be fully sunsetted. DDA uses machine learning to credit touchpoints based on their actual impact.

entries.rule: AI Overviews in Google Search are reducing organic CTR and pushing more demand to paid placements, driving CPC inflation of 10-20% YoY across most verticals. Position 1 paid ads may see higher CTR as AI Overviews push organic results further down.
entries.notes: AI Overviews rollout is ongoing and impact varies by query type and vertical. The effect on paid vs organic CTR distribution is still being measured.

entries.rule: Under Smart Bidding (tCPA, tROAS, Maximize Conversions, Maximize Conversion Value), manual device bid adjustments are overridden — Smart Bidding adjusts bids by device automatically per auction. Only Manual CPC and eCPC respect device bid adjustments.
entries.notes: The -100% mobile bid adjustment still works to exclude mobile entirely, even under Smart Bidding. As Smart Bidding adoption approaches universal, device bid adjustments become less relevant.

entries.rule: PMax campaigns can serve on Search inventory alongside Search campaigns. When both target the same queries, exact match keywords in Search campaigns take priority over PMax for identical queries. Behavior for broad/phrase is less clear.
entries.notes: The priority rules between PMax and Search campaigns evolve. Google has stated exact match priority but the behavior for broad/phrase may change.

entries.rule: Google Ads CPCs have been rising 10-20% year-over-year (2024-2026) due to increased competition, AI-driven bidding adoption, and SERP layout changes (AI Overviews reducing organic clicks). Adjust historical benchmarks upward accordingly.
entries.notes: Inflation rate varies by vertical. Some verticals (legal, insurance) see higher inflation. This rate could accelerate or decelerate.

entries.rule: Google's bidding engine uses an LLM-based auction system evaluating hundreds of real-time signals per auction: device type, precise location, time of day, audience segments, query intent, competitive density, browser, OS, and more. No human bidder can access or process these signals.
entries.notes: Google describes their bidding as AI/ML-powered. The 'LLM-based' characterization reflects the current generation. The signal set and model architecture evolve continuously.
